concepts.name: Euler Totient Function
concepts.definition: φ(n) counts the positive integers up to n that are relatively prime to n
concepts.formula: φ(n) = n ∏(1 - 1/p) for all prime p dividing n

concepts.name: Fermat's Little Theorem
concepts.definition: If p is prime and a is not divisible by p, then a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p)

concepts.name: RSA Cryptosystem
concepts.definition: Public-key cryptosystem based on difficulty of factoring large integers

concepts.name: Modular Inverse
concepts.definition: Integer a has modular inverse b mod m if ab ≡ 1 (mod m)

concepts.name: Greatest Common Divisor
concepts.definition: Largest positive integer that divides both a and b

concepts.name: Mersenne Prime
concepts.definition: Prime number of form 2^p - 1 where p is prime

concepts.name: Twin Primes
concepts.definition: Pair of primes that differ by 2, e.g., (3,5), (5,7), (11,13)

concepts.name: Chinese Remainder Theorem
concepts.definition: System of congruences with pairwise coprime moduli has unique solution

concepts.name: Group
concepts.definition: Set with associative binary operation, identity element, and inverses

concepts.name: Ring
concepts.definition: Algebraic structure with two binary operations (addition and multiplication)

concepts.name: Field
concepts.definition: Ring where every non-zero element has multiplicative inverse

concepts.name: Homomorphism
concepts.definition: Structure-preserving map between algebraic structures

concepts.name: Isomorphism
concepts.definition: Bijective homomorphism that preserves structure completely

concepts.name: Quotient Group
concepts.definition: Group formed by partitioning group by normal subgroup
concepts.notation: G/N where N is normal subgroup of G

concepts.name: Galois Theory
concepts.definition: Connection between field theory and group theory for polynomial equations

concepts.name: Ideal
concepts.definition: Special subset of ring closed under ring multiplication and addition

concepts.name: Sylow Theorems
concepts.definition: Theorems about existence and properties of p-subgroups in finite groups

concepts.name: Fundamental Theorem of Algebra
concepts.definition: Every non-constant polynomial over complex numbers has at least one root

concepts.name: Homeomorphism
concepts.definition: Continuous bijection with continuous inverse between topological spaces

concepts.name: Compactness
concepts.definition: Topological property generalizing closed and bounded in Euclidean space

concepts.name: Connectedness
concepts.definition: Topological space that cannot be partitioned into disjoint non-empty open sets

concepts.name: Metric Space
concepts.definition: Set with distance function satisfying triangle inequality

concepts.name: Hausdorff Space
concepts.definition: Topological space where distinct points have disjoint neighborhoods

concepts.name: Open Set
concepts.definition: Set where every point has neighborhood contained in the set

concepts.name: Closed Set
concepts.definition: Set whose complement is open, or equivalently, contains all its limit points

concepts.name: Fundamental Group
concepts.definition: Group of homotopy classes of loops based at a point

concepts.name: Homotopy
concepts.definition: Continuous deformation between continuous maps

concepts.name: Topological Invariant
concepts.definition: Property preserved under homeomorphisms

concepts.name: Linear Programming
concepts.definition: Optimization of linear objective function subject to linear constraints
concepts.standard_form: minimize c^T x subject to Ax = b, x ≥ 0

concepts.name: Simplex Algorithm
concepts.definition: Method for solving linear programming problems by moving along polytope vertices

concepts.name: Convexity
concepts.definition: Property where line segment between any two points lies entirely within set

concepts.name: Lagrange Multipliers
concepts.definition: Method for finding extrema of function subject to equality constraints

concepts.name: KKT Conditions
concepts.definition: Necessary conditions for optimality in constrained optimization

concepts.name: Gradient Descent
concepts.definition: Iterative optimization algorithm using negative gradient direction

concepts.name: Duality Theory
concepts.definition: Relationship between primal and dual optimization problems

concepts.name: Dynamic Programming
concepts.definition: Method solving complex problems by breaking into simpler subproblems

concepts.name: Interior Point Methods
concepts.definition: Algorithms solving optimization by moving through interior of feasible region

concepts.name: Quadratic Programming
concepts.definition: Optimization with quadratic objective and linear constraints
concepts.form: minimize (1/2)x^T Q x + c^T x subject to Ax ≤ b

concepts.name: Predicate Logic
concepts.definition: Formal system with quantifiers, predicates, and logical connectives

concepts.name: Propositional Logic
concepts.definition: Logic of propositions connected by logical connectives

concepts.name: Gödel's Incompleteness Theorems
concepts.definition: Fundamental limitations of formal systems in mathematics

concepts.name: Decidability
concepts.definition: Property of decision problems having algorithmic solution

concepts.name: Halting Problem
concepts.definition: Undecidable problem of determining if program halts on given input

concepts.name: Church-Turing Thesis
concepts.definition: Effectively calculable functions are exactly Turing-computable functions

concepts.name: Model Theory
concepts.definition: Study of mathematical structures that satisfy formal theories

concepts.name: Proof Theory
concepts.definition: Study of proofs as formal mathematical objects

concepts.name: Modal Logic
concepts.definition: Extension of propositional logic with necessity and possibility operators

concepts.name: Computational Complexity
concepts.definition: Study of resources required for solving computational problems

relations.description: Fermat's Little Theorem is fundamental to RSA key generation and correctness
Euler's totient function determines RSA key sizes and security
Extended Euclidean algorithm computes modular inverses
RSA decryption requires computing modular inverse of encryption exponent
CRT speeds up RSA decryption through parallel computation
Using Mersenne primes in RSA can create security vulnerabilities
Both sequences count important combinatorial objects
Ring theory generalizes group theory by adding multiplication
Field is special case of ring with multiplicative inverses
Isomorphism is bijective homomorphism
Quotient groups are constructed from groups and normal subgroups
Galois theory studies field extensions and their automorphism groups
Ideals are fundamental structures within rings
Sylow theorems classify subgroups in finite groups
Fundamental Theorem characterizes complex field as algebraically closed
Galois theory provides elegant proof of Fundamental Theorem
Metric spaces naturally define topology through open balls
Closed sets are complements of open sets
Compactness defined through open covers
In metric spaces, compactness equivalent to complete and totally bounded
Fundamental group measures connectedness algebraically
Homeomorphisms preserve all topological invariants
Fundamental group defined as homotopy classes of loops
Compact Hausdorff spaces have many desirable properties
Every metric space is Hausdorff
Connectedness is preserved under homeomorphisms
Simplex algorithm is primary method for solving linear programs
Linear programming problems have convex feasible regions
KKT conditions extend Lagrange multipliers to inequality constraints
Convexity ensures gradient descent finds global optimum
Linear programming demonstrates strong duality clearly
Interior point methods solve systems satisfying KKT conditions
Dynamic programming solves optimization with optimal substructure
Quadratic programming extends linear programming to quadratic objectives
Convex quadratic programs can be solved efficiently
Interior point methods provide polynomial-time alternative to simplex
Predicate logic extends propositional logic with quantifiers
Gödel's theorems show inherent limitations of predicate logic systems
Halting problem is canonical undecidable problem
Church-Turing thesis makes halting problem meaningful
Incompleteness theorems show some mathematical statements are undecidable
Model theory studies semantic interpretation of predicate logic
Proof theory studies syntactic aspects of predicate logic
Modal logic adds necessity/possibility operators to propositional logic
Complexity theory quantifies resources needed for decidable problems
Undecidability motivates study of tractable computational problems
RSA security relies on computational complexity of factoring
Euler's totient counts elements in multiplicative group mod n
Fermat's theorem follows from Lagrange's theorem in group theory
Fibonacci search is optimization algorithm using Fibonacci ratios
Catalan numbers efficiently computed using dynamic programming
Metric spaces provide distance structure for optimization algorithms
Continuous functions on compact sets attain their extrema
Connected domains ensure continuity of optimization paths
Homeomorphic domains have equivalent optimization landscapes
Galois theory explains why general polynomials cannot be solved by radicals
Ideal theory in logic relates to Gödel's construction methods
Twin prime conjecture represents deep computational complexity question
Mersenne numbers have efficient primality testing algorithms
Sylow theorems lead to algorithms for analyzing finite groups
Fundamental theorem leads to polynomial root-finding algorithms
Hausdorff property ensures uniqueness of optimization limits
Homotopy methods continuously deform optimization problems
Both theories exemplify fundamental duality principles in mathematics
Interior point methods use geometric properties of constraint surfaces
Modal logic can be interpreted using topological spaces
Model theory studies interpretations of algebraic theories
Proof theory analyzes computational complexity of generating proofs
CRT is fundamental result about products of rings
Quotient groups naturally arise from group homomorphisms
Isomorphisms in topology preserve all topological invariants
Gradient descent follows steepest descent paths on manifolds
Lagrange multipliers naturally extend to optimization on manifolds
Fundamental groups are examples of groups arising from topology
Open sets are fundamental examples of measurable sets
Closed sets relate to completeness in functional analysis
